name: pyscroll

metadata.summary: Fast scrolling maps library for pygame

documentation: see readme.md

methods.name: pyscroll.animation.AnimationToken.__init__
documentation:
Constructor

Args:
    positions: Set of positions where the tile is on the map
    frames: Sequence of frames that compromise the animation
    initial_time: Used to compensate time between starting and changing animations

methods.name: pyscroll.animation.AnimationToken.advance
documentation:
Advance the frame, and set timer for next frame

Timer value is calculated by adding last_time and the
duration of the next frame

The next frame is returned

* This API may change in the future

Args:
    last_time: Duration of the last frame

classes.name: pyscroll.group.PyscrollGroup

methods.name: pyscroll.group.PyscrollGroup.center
documentation:
Center the group/map on a pixel.

The basemap and all sprites will be realigned to draw correctly.
Centering the map will not change the rect of the sprites.

Args:
    value: x, y coordinates to center the camera on

methods.name: pyscroll.group.PyscrollGroup.view
documentation: Return a Rect representing visible portion of map.

methods.name: pyscroll.group.PyscrollGroup.draw
documentation:
Draw map and all sprites onto the surface.

Args:
    surface: Surface to draw to

Layered Group with ability to center sprites and scrolling map.

Args:
    map_layer: Pyscroll Renderer

functions.name: pyscroll.common.rect_difference
documentation: Compute difference of two rects.  Returns up to 4.

classes.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.__init__
documentation:
Constructor

NOTE: `colorkey` and `alpha` are special purpose and not related
      to the transparency values for tiles. In 99.99% of cases,
      you do not need to set them

Args:
    data: reference to data source
    size: if true, cannot scroll past map edge
    clamp_camera: determines how tile animations are processed
    colorkey: used for enabling transparent layers.  not needed usually
    alpha: used for enabling transparent layers.  not needed usually
    time_source: used to get time when cycling tile animations
    scaling_function: what function to use when scaling the zoom buffer
    tall_sprites: deprecated.  will be removed in future
    sprite_damage_height: modify when sprites are drawn over tiles on another layer
    zoom: negative numbers make map smaller, positive numbers zoom in

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.reload
documentation: Reload tiles and animations for the data source.

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.scroll
documentation:
Scroll the background in pixels.

Args:
    vector: x, y

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.center
documentation:
Center the map on a pixel.

Float numbers will be rounded.

Args:
    coords: x, y

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.draw
documentation:
Draw the map onto a surface.

pass a rect that defines the draw area for:
    drawing to an area smaller that the whole window/screen

surfaces may optionally be passed that will be blitted onto the surface.
this must be a sequence of tuples containing a layer number, image, and
rect in screen coordinates.  surfaces will be drawn in order passed,
and will be correctly drawn with tiles from a higher layer overlapping
the surface.

surfaces list should be in the following format:
[ (layer, surface, rect), ... ]

or this:
[ (layer, surface, rect, blendmode_flags), ... ]

Args:
    surface: surface to draw to
    rect: area to draw to
    surfaces: optional sequence of surfaces to interlace between tiles
    rect: area that was drawn over

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.zoom
documentation:
Zoom the map in or out.

Increase this number to make map appear to come closer to camera.
Decrease this number to make map appear to move away from camera.

Default value is 1.0
This value cannot be negative or 0.0

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.set_size
documentation:
Set the size of the map in pixels.

This is an expensive operation, do only when absolutely needed.

Args:
    size: pixel size of camera/view of the group

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.redraw_tiles
documentation:
Redraw the visible portion of the buffer -- it is slow.

Args:
    surface: where to draw

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.get_center_offset
documentation: Return x, y pair that will change world coords to screen coords.

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.translate_point
documentation:
Translate world coordinates and return screen coordinates.

Args:
    point: point to translate

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.translate_rect
documentation:
Translate rect position and size to screen coordinates.

Args:
    rect: rect to translate

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.translate_points
documentation:
Translate coordinates and return screen coordinates.

Args:
    points: points to translate

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer.translate_rects
documentation:
Translate rect position and size to screen coordinates.

Args:
    rects: rects to translate

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._render_map
documentation:
Render the map and optional surfaces to destination surface.

Args:
    surface: pygame surface to draw to
    rect: area to draw to
    surfaces: optional sequence of surfaces to interlace between tiles

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._clear_surface
documentation:
Clear the surface using the right clear color.

Args:
    surface: surface to clear
    area: area to clear

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._draw_surfaces
documentation:
Draw surfaces while correcting overlapping tile layers.

Args:
    surface: destination
    offset: offset to compensate for buffer alignment
    surfaces: sequence of surfaces to blit

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._queue_edge_tiles
documentation:
Queue edge tiles and clear edge areas on buffer if needed.

Args:
    dx: Edge along X axis to enqueue
    dy: Edge along Y axis to enqueue

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._create_buffers
documentation:
Create the buffers, taking in account pixel alpha or colorkey.

Args:
    view_size: pixel size of the view
    buffer_size: pixel size of the buffer

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._initialize_buffers
documentation:
Create the buffers to cache tile drawing.

Args:
    view_size: size of the draw area

methods.name: pyscroll.orthographic.BufferedRenderer._flush_tile_queue
documentation:
Blit the queued tiles and block until the tile queue is empty.

Args:
    surface: surface to draw onto

Renderer that support scrolling, zooming, layers, and animated tiles

The buffered renderer must be used with a data class to get tile,
shape, and animation information.  See the data class api in
pyscroll.data, or use the built-in pytmx support for loading maps
created with Tiled.

methods.name: pyscroll.isometric.IsometricBufferedRenderer._flush_tile_queue
documentation: Blits (x, y, layer) tuples to buffer from iterator

methods.name: pyscroll.isometric.IsometricBufferedRenderer.center
documentation: center the map on a "map pixel"

TEST ISOMETRIC

here be dragons.  lots of odd, untested, and unoptimised stuff.

- coalescing of surfaces is not supported
- drawing may have depth sorting issues

modules.name: pyscroll.data
documentation:
This file contains a few classes for accessing data

If you are developing your own map format, please use this
as a template.  Just fill in values that work for your game.

classes.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.process_animation_queue
documentation:
Given the time and the tile view, process tile changes and return them

Args:
    tile_view: Rect representing tiles on the screen

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter._update_time
documentation:
Update the internal clock.

This may change in future versions.

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.prepare_tiles
documentation:
Somewhat experimental: The renderer will advise data layer of its view

For some data providers, it would be useful to know what tiles will be drawn
before they are ready to draw.  This exposes the tile view to the data.

* A draw will happen immediately after this returns.
* Do not hold on to this reference or change it.

Args:
    tiles: Reference to the tile view

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.reload_animations
documentation:
Reload animation information.

PyscrollDataAdapter.get_animations must be implemented

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.get_tile_image
documentation:
Get a tile image, respecting current animations.

Args:
    x: x coordinate
    y: y coordinate
    l: layer

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter._get_tile_image
documentation:
Return tile at the coordinates, or None is empty.

This is used to query the data source directly, without
checking for animations or any other tile transformations.

You must override this to support other data sources

Args:
    x: x coordinate
    y: y coordinate
    l: layer

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter._get_tile_image_by_id
documentation:
Return Image by a custom ID.

Used for animations.  Not required for static maps.

Args:
    id:

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.get_animations
documentation:
Get tile animation data.

This method is subject to change in the future.

Must yield tuples that in the following format:
  ( ID, Frames )

  Where Frames is:
  [ (ID, Duration), ... ]

  And ID is a reference to a tile image.
  This will be something accessible using _get_tile_image_by_id

  Duration should be in milliseconds

methods.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.get_tile_images_by_rect
documentation:
Given a 2d area, return generator of tile images inside.

Given the coordinates, yield the following tuple for each tile:
  X, Y, Layer Number, pygame Surface

This method also defines render order by re arranging the
positions of each tile as it is yielded to the renderer.

There is an optimization that you can make for your data:
If you can provide access to tile information in a batch,
then pyscroll can access data faster and render quicker.

To implement this optimization, override this method.

Not like python 'Range': should include the end index!

Args:
    rect: Rect-like object that defines tiles to draw

class_variables.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.tile_size
documentation: (int, int): size of each tile in pixels

class_variables.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.map_size
documentation: (int, int): size of map in tiles

class_variables.name: pyscroll.data.PyscrollDataAdapter.visible_tile_layers
documentation: list of visible layer integers

Use this as a template for data adapters

Contains logic for handling animated tiles.  Animated tiles
are a WIP feature, and while in theory will work with any data
source, it is only tested using Tiled maps, loaded with pytmx.

documentation: Required for sprite collation - not implemented

methods.name: pyscroll.data.MapAggregator._get_tile_image_by_id

methods.name: pyscroll.data.MapAggregator.add_map

Add map data and position it with an offset

Args:

methods.name: pyscroll.data.MapAggregator.remove_map
documentation: Remove map - not implemented

methods.name: pyscroll.data.MapAggregator.get_animations
documentation: Get animations - not implemented

methods.name: pyscroll.data.MapAggregator.reload_data
documentation: Reload the tiles - not implemented

Combine multiple data sources with an offset

Currently this is just in a test phase.

Has the following limitations:
- Tile sizes must be the same for all maps
- No tile animations
- Sprites cannot be under layers
- Cannot remove maps once added

modules.name: pyscroll.quadtree
documentation:
Two classes for quadtree collision detection.

A quadtree is used with pyscroll to detect overlapping tiles.

classes.name: pyscroll.quadtree.FastQuadTree

methods.name: pyscroll.quadtree.FastQuadTree.__init__
documentation:
Creates a quad-tree.

Args:
    items: Sequence of items to check
    depth: The maximum recursion depth
    boundary: The bounding rectangle of all of the items in the quad-tree

methods.name: pyscroll.quadtree.FastQuadTree.hit
documentation:
Returns the items that overlap a bounding rectangle.

Returns the set of all items in the quad-tree that overlap with a
bounding rectangle.

Args:
    rect: The bounding rectangle being tested

An implementation of a quad-tree.

This faster version of the quadtree class is tuned for pygame's rect
objects, or objects with a rect attribute.  The return value will always
be a set of a tuples that represent the items passed.  In other words,
you will not get back the objects that were passed, just a tuple that
describes it.

Items being stored in the tree must be a pygame.Rect or have have a
.rect (pygame.Rect) attribute that is a pygame.Rect

original code from https://pygame.org/wiki/QuadTree
